reason: MCQ with papers provided. Not closed-book solvable: options B and D are marked Correct but require paper-specific information not stated in the stem (non-uniform kinetic-parameter rise specifically from 50% to 90% SOC; interpretation that both activation energy and pre-exponential factor are higher at 100% SOC). The stem only gives onset-temperature and max temperature-rate observations, which do not uniquely imply those kinetic-parameter conclusions without the paper’s kinetic-parameter findings. This creates hidden-dependency inadmissibility.

Closed-book solvability fails for at least one Correct-labeled option: D asserts a specific causal mechanism (higher heating power makes cell 1 complete thermal runaway more rapidly, reducing heat exposure duration to cell 2) that is not stated in the stem and is not a necessary inference from the provided observations; alternative explanations (e.g., different heat-loss partitioning to the heater/fixtures or altered temperature gradients/trigger timing) could also fit the same TRPI and Tmax trends. Because D’s correctness depends on paper-specific stage/duration details not given in the stem, the item is inadmissible.

MCQ with papers provided. The stem contains all needed quantitative/qualitative facts (Texo range, delay-time variability at similar Texo, one-step model bounds spanning ~order of magnitude with all data inside, reactant consumption up to 25% above 120°C, and ~2× delay-time elongation when accounting for consumption). Correct options A, D, and F follow from general reasoning about predictor sufficiency and condition-dependent model corrections, without requiring any paper-only definitions. Incorrect options B, E, and G are clearly overclaims given the stated uncertainty bounds, and C contradicts the stated direction of the correction (accounting for consumption elongates delay time). No contradictions with the evidence bundle.

Short-answer; papers provided. The stem contains the observed phenomena and asks for physical mechanisms. The gold answer’s core claims (pressure-driven bleed valve venting causing a dT/dt drop at the Stage II–III transition; increased heating power lengthening TRPI and lowering cell-2 Tmax via reduced effective heat exposure/transfer from cell 1) are coherent, non-ambiguous, and do not require unstated paper-only thresholds or stage definitions beyond what is described. Evidence explicitly supports bleed-valve opening at a pressure threshold causing the Stage III temperature-rate decrease and supports the counterintuitive trend that higher heating power increases TRPI while reducing cell-2 maximum temperature, with a plausible mechanism consistent with general thermal reasoning. No contradictions found.
